title: Economic Impact Supported by Booking.com in Europe
gist: A Tourism Economics (Oxford Economics) study finds travel booked through Booking.com supported EUR 691 billion in total economic output, EUR 344 billion in GDP, and 4.7 million jobs across Europe in 2025, with over half of visitor spending reaching businesses outside hospitality.

themes.theme: Total economic footprint of Booking.com travel in Europe

insights.claim: Travel booked through Booking.com supported EUR 691 billion in total economic output across Europe in 2025, including EUR 344 billion in GDP, 4.7 million jobs, EUR 175 billion in wages, and EUR 137 billion in tax revenues.
insights.speaker: Geena Bevenour
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of online travel intermediation as a macro driver of European GDP, jobs, and tax revenue, relevant to OTA platform valuation and tourism-exposed economies.
insights.quote: Booking.com-linked travel supporting €691 billion in total economic output across Europe.

insights.claim: Travellers who booked their trip through Booking.com spent EUR 291 billion during their stays in 2025, and this direct spending supported EUR 152 billion in GDP and nearly 2.5 million jobs.
insights.speaker: Geena Bevenour
insights.so_what: Shows the direct spending base before multiplier effects, a baseline for sizing the consumer travel wallet captured via OTAs.
insights.quote: Travellers who booked their trip through Booking.com spent €291 billion during their stays.

insights.claim: More than half of visitor spending tied to Booking.com travel in Europe reached businesses beyond hospitality, including retail, transport, and entertainment, even though hospitality was the largest single category.
insights.speaker: Geena Bevenour
insights.so_what: Tourism demand is a broad-based consumer tailwind across multiple sectors, not just hotels, widening the set of investable beneficiaries.
insights.quote: more than half of visitor spending reached businesses beyond hospitality

insights.claim: Supplier purchases, employee wage spending, and cross-border trade effects generated additional economic activity that lifted the total contribution of Booking.com-linked travel to EUR 691 billion in output, well above the EUR 291 billion in direct visitor spending.
insights.speaker: Geena Bevenour
insights.so_what: Indicates a large indirect/induced multiplier on travel spending, supporting the case that tourism shocks ripple widely through European supply chains.
insights.quote: Supplier purchases, employee wage spending, and cross-border trade effects, generated additional economic activity
